prompt:
We print short claim codes on physical gift cards and support keeps fielding 'code doesn't work' calls that turn out to be someone reading O as 0 or typing an 8 for a B. I want our own codec, b32check.py, so a mistyped code is REJECTED with a clear error instead of silently resolving to some other customer's blob. No base64 module tricks that get the alphabet wrong — build it.

Alphabet is Crockford-style base32: 0123456789ABCDEFGHJKMNPQRSTVWXYZ (no I, L, O, U). `encode(data: bytes) -> str` packs bits RFC-4648 style: bytes big-endian into 5-bit groups, last group zero-padded, one symbol per group, NO '=' padding. Then append exactly one check symbol: the payload bytes interpreted as a big-endian integer, mod 37, indexed into the alphabet extended with *~$=U (so checks can be symbols data never uses). Empty input still gets its check symbol: encode(b"") == "0".

`decode(text) -> bytes` reverses it, with human-friendly normalization first: case-insensitive, hyphens ignored, I and L read as 1, O as 0. After normalization it must be strict: unknown symbols, a *~$=U anywhere but the final position, payload lengths that can't come from whole bytes, NONZERO padding bits (that's how we catch a class of typos the checksum alone can't), and check-symbol mismatches all raise. Exceptions: DecodeError(ValueError), and ChecksumError(DecodeError) specifically when the structure was fine but the check symbol disagrees.

The property I actually care about, and the tests hammer it: any single-symbol substitution and adjacent transposition in a code must fail to decode. That's the whole reason for mod 37 rather than mod 32 — do not 'simplify' it.

`python3 test_b32check.py` is the gate. Tests stay as-is. Stdlib only.
